hero.title: Frontend engineer building AI-integrated products.
hero.subtitle: React · TypeScript · AI Agents · Developer Tools
hero.bio: A decade of turning ambiguity into shipped software. Deep frontend craft, working backend fluency, and hands-on AI engineering — RAG, tool calling, guardrails — proven across 30+ products I've built and shipped solo.

projects.title: git-scope
projects.description: A fast terminal UI to see the status of every Git repository on your machine. Contribution graphs, time-machine history, and batch operations — distributed via Homebrew.
highlights: 98+ stars and 15 forks — scans 50+ repositories with fuzzy search and workspace switching
Contribution graph visualization and time-machine commit navigation in the terminal
15 tagged releases with cross-platform Homebrew distribution

caseStudy.problem: Developers accumulate dozens of Git repositories on one machine — work projects, side projects, forks, experiments. Finding which ones have uncommitted changes, unpushed commits, or stale branches means cd-ing into each and running git status by hand. There was no fast way to see the state of every repo at once without leaving the terminal.
caseStudy.approach: git-scope is a terminal UI that scans every Git repository under a directory and shows their status in a single view. Built in Go with Bubble Tea, it runs as one dependency-free binary, starts instantly, and stays in the terminal where developers already work. Fuzzy search and workspace switching keep 50+ repos navigable, and an inline contribution graph plus time-machine view bring history browsing into the same window.

decisions.title: A TUI, not a web dashboard
decisions.detail: A terminal UI ships as one static binary — no runtime, no server, no browser tab. It launches in milliseconds and works over SSH, which is the right home for a tool you reach for between commands.

decisions.title: Local-only, zero telemetry
decisions.detail: git-scope reads the local filesystem and never phones home — no account, no daemon, no network calls. That keeps it trustworthy for scanning private and client repositories.

decisions.title: Homebrew + tagged releases
decisions.detail: Distributing via Homebrew across 15 cross-platform releases made install and upgrade a one-liner — the friction drop that turned early interest into repeat users.

caseStudy.outcome: git-scope reached 98 GitHub stars across 15 releases and is in daily use scanning 50+ repositories at a glance. It validated that a focused, local-first terminal tool can find real adoption with no backend and no web app.

projects.title: RivalEye
projects.description: Competitive intelligence SaaS that monitors competitor pricing, tech stack, and branding across 4 global regions — every change arrives as an AI tactical brief, not just a notification.
highlights: Full-stack Next.js 16 + React 19 app with Supabase Postgres, RLS auth, and Trigger.dev background jobs
3-scraper fallback pipeline (Firecrawl, Playwright, Cheerio) with Gemini Vision for branding analysis
Geo-aware price monitoring from 4 regions plus competitor Core Web Vitals tracking

caseStudy.problem: Founders and product teams track competitors by manually re-checking their sites — pricing pages, changelogs, landing pages. Regional price tests, quiet tech-stack changes, and rebranding signals slip through unnoticed. Generic page monitors exist, but they send raw 'this page changed' diffs: all noise, no meaning, and no answer to the only question that matters — what should I do about it?
caseStudy.approach: RivalEye scans competitor sites daily from 4 regions (US, EU, India, Global), diffs pricing, tech stack, and branding, and turns each meaningful change into an AI tactical brief — what happened, why it matters, what to do next. It's a complete production SaaS shipped solo: Supabase Postgres with RLS auth, Trigger.dev background jobs, a 3-scraper crawling pipeline with Gemini Vision analysis, Dodo Payments billing with plan limits and quotas, and alerting via email and Slack.

decisions.title: A 3-scraper fallback pipeline
decisions.detail: Scraping is the flakiest part of any monitoring product — sites block bots, render client-side, or change structure. RivalEye tries Firecrawl first, falls back to Playwright, then Cheerio, so one vendor outage or one hostile site never silences monitoring for a competitor.

decisions.title: Meaningfulness before notification
decisions.detail: Noise kills monitoring tools. A diff engine with meaningfulness checks and alert rules decides whether a change deserves attention at all; only then does the AI generate a tactical brief. Users get a handful of briefs worth reading, not a feed of trivial DOM changes.

decisions.title: Vision AI for branding, not DOM diffs
decisions.detail: Repositioning shows up visually — logo tweaks, palette shifts, typography changes — long before it shows up in markup. Screenshots stored in Cloudflare R2 are analyzed by Gemini Vision, catching signals a text diff can't see.

caseStudy.outcome: RivalEye is live at rivaleye.vercel.app with a free tier and a $49/mo Pro plan. As a solo build it covers the full surface of a production SaaS — auth, multi-tenant data isolation, billing, background jobs, abuse detection, and a Vitest suite — and proves out an AI product thesis: the value isn't detecting change, it's deciding which changes matter and saying what to do about them.

projects.title: ai-cost-guard
projects.description: Open-source TypeScript guardrails for AI applications — block risky prompts, enforce budgets before model calls, and control multi-tenant LLM spend. Published on npm.
highlights: Pre-call budget enforcement with hard/soft limits and tenant-level quotas for multi-tenant SaaS
Secret and PII detection that blocks risky prompts before they reach the model
Webhook alerts, spend tracking, and ROI reports — deployable via Docker or Kubernetes

projects.title: Recall
projects.description: Local-first command memory for developers. Captures terminal commands with repo context so you can find exactly what you ran last time — with AI-powered search, no cloud required.
highlights: Repo-aware capture: every command stored with cwd, repo, and timestamp context in SQLite
AI-powered semantic search plus project memory that rehydrates 'how we run this project'
Strict privacy controls — pause capture, ignore sensitive patterns, delete locally, zero telemetry

caseStudy.problem: Terminal work is high-leverage and strangely ephemeral. Developers re-google commands they know they've run before, forget the first three commands after cloning each repo, and repeat the same workflows across projects. Shell history technically has the answers, but it's a flat list of strings — no repo context, no timestamps that mean anything, no way to ask 'how do I run this project?'
caseStudy.approach: Recall hooks into the shell and captures every command with its context — repo, working directory, timestamp — into a local SQLite database. Full-text search finds the exact command you ran last time, scoped to the repo you're in; project memory rehydrates 'how we run this project' from startup patterns and recent commands. An optional AI layer adds semantic search on top, but the CLI is fully useful without it. Distributed via a Homebrew tap with an auto-updated formula.

decisions.title: Local-first is the product, not a feature
decisions.detail: Shell history is some of the most sensitive data a developer has — tokens, hostnames, file paths. Recall stores everything in local SQLite with no accounts, no cloud sync, and no telemetry. A command-memory tool that phones home would never earn the trust it needs to be left running.

decisions.title: AI is opt-in, search works without it
decisions.detail: Semantic search only activates when the user explicitly configures API keys or a local model. Full-text search over structured context covers most lookups with zero setup and zero network calls — the AI layer is an upgrade, not a dependency. No AI feature runs without explicit opt-in.

decisions.title: Capture with an off switch, everywhere
decisions.detail: A tool that records commands sits one bad default away from being a keylogger. Recall ships with pause capture, ignore rules for sensitive command patterns, local deletion, and a two-mode hook install that shows you exactly what's added to your shell rc — nothing is appended without consent.

caseStudy.outcome: Recall shipped as a public beta on Homebrew with capture, repo-aware search, and project memory production-ready, while experimental features (workflow replay, background embedding) stay gated behind a flag. It's a working answer to a daily developer problem — and a case study in shipping AI-adjacent tooling where privacy constraints shape the architecture instead of being bolted on.

experience.description: In 2021 I left consulting to build products end-to-end — I wanted to own every decision from architecture to launch, not one slice of a delivery pipeline. Since then I've designed, built, and shipped 30+ products solo: git-scope (98+ GitHub stars, Homebrew distribution), npm libraries (ai-cost-guard, loopkit, debugg), and production SaaS like RivalEye — auth, billing, background jobs, and AI pipelines included. Five years of shipping alone taught me velocity, ruthless scope-cutting, and daily hands-on AI engineering: agents, RAG, guardrails. Now I'm looking to bring that end-to-end speed to a team building something bigger than one person can.

Built an enterprise Code Scan Platform from scratch that identified vulnerabilities in large-scale applications. Reduced deployment time by 60% with CI/CD pipelines and delivered pixel-perfect React dashboards from Figma designs.

Delivered secure banking features for national banks. Improved backend performance by 40% with Redis caching and PostgreSQL query optimization. Converted Figma wireframes into production-grade UIs in Agile sprints.
